answer: The Vrhaspatitattva defines the relationship of cetana (consciousness) and acetana (the unconscious) in Chapter 1 (the sole chapter), around verse 6 and its prose commentary. The text states: 'The supreme tattva is twofold: cetana and acetana. It pervades all tattvas, is subtle, and must be discerned with effort.' The prose then elaborates: cetana (Conscious Principle) is that which has the nature of knowledge, which knows, is never subject to forgetting, is eternally aware at all times, and is unobscured. Acetana (Unconscious Principle) is that which is without knowledge, like a stone. Crucially, their relationship is generative: when cetana and acetana meet, they give rise to all the tattvas — pradhānatattva, triguṇatattva, buddhitattva, ahaṅkāratattva, bāhyendriyatattva, karmendriyatattva, and pañcamahābhūtatattva. The text further clarifies the asymmetry of their relationship elsewhere: acetana is willed into being by cetana — the conscious acts as a potter who shapes the unconscious (clay) according to its will. Work slug: unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28, chapter 1, paragraphs p-04d69c and p-c31df3.
